chapters.title: A clear vision, or a slow failure
chapters.text:
A mission describes what your organization does today. A vision describes what you want it to be tomorrow, and the road that gets you there. Mission sets the rules for the daily work by defining who you are and what you cover. A strong mission statement answers four questions, which are what defines us, who we serve, which problem we tackle, and what change we want to make. Those four answers shape the identity of the organization in the present.

Vision, on the other hand, asks you to picture the future. It covers what you want to see, and what you want to build, across the whole of your company. The clearer the vision, the easier it is to keep the team moving in the same direction.

Building your own vision script. A vision script is one of the most useful things a leader can write, and the process matters as much as the result. There are three steps that work for most people.

Step one, get away and clear your head. Find a quiet place where nobody will interrupt you. The vision you are trying to build is a picture of the future, and that kind of thinking needs space.

Step two, believe that your best is still ahead of you. A positive frame of mind is the soil that a vision grows in. Hold on to the idea that the future will give you what you ask for, even if you are only asking in your own head.

A change in how you think is the first step toward a change in what you see.

Step three, picture the future and put it into words. Let your mind wander. Build clear, vivid mental images of the things you want. After a few minutes, the pictures will start to come on their own. When the images are clear, write them down. Then write the steps that will get you there. The vision and the plan belong on the same page.

A small note worth tucking away, Walt Disney was once fired from the Kansas City Star because his editor said he had no good ideas. The rest of the story is history.

chapters.title: How a clear vision can save a company
chapters.text:
Michael Hyatt had worked for two publishers before he decided to start his own. He and his friend and business partner, Robert Wolfer, had the drive and the skills to make it work.

They began with five titles in 1989, and not long after they landed a meeting with the former baseball pitcher Orel Hershiser. They were excited about publishing his book, but they did not have the cash to do it. They called one of their investors, asked for a loan, and put up shares in their company as collateral. They put the book together in less than a hundred days, and Out of the Blue sat on the New York Times bestseller list for several weeks in 1989. The win let them pay back the loan and grow the business.

For a while, they published every kind of book that came through the door. They had no clear target, and they grabbed every opportunity. It worked until they tried to take a big step and sign with a major distributor. Hyatt and his partner joined a large sales company with a real staff, and the first month under the new deal brought in less than they had been making on their own. After four years and several more loans, the publishing house collapsed. They lost everything, even the office chairs, and it happened because they had not built a clear vision.

Drive, ideas, and confidence are all useful, but without a vision, the rest will not save you.

The traps of running a business without a vision are easy to name. The first is being unprepared for the future. Kodak sat on top of the camera market for years, but the company was not ready for the digital shift, and the shift ate the business alive. The second is missed opportunities. A team without a vision is a team that lets the right chance go by. The third is scattered priorities. Without a vision, the team has no shared push to move forward.

Other traps include bad strategic calls, wasted money, wasted time, and founders burning out before the work is done. A clear vision is the engine that keeps a company moving, and it can be updated as the world changes. A company without a real vision will sit still, and a company that sits still will eventually disappear.

chapters.title: A simple way to test your own clarity
chapters.text:
Clarity brings calm, and calm brings confidence, but a clear vision is hard to build because a vision is, by definition, a picture of something that does not yet exist. When you are writing yours, watch out for vague language. The more specific you can make the picture, the easier it is for your team to see it with you.

It is normal to struggle with clarity, and there is a tool that can help. It is called a vision grid, and it is divided into four quadrants. In Quadrant 1, the vision is still in the leader's head, and the team has not picked it up. In Quadrant 2, the vision is starting to take shape for the leader, but only a few followers see it. In Quadrant 3, the leader thinks the team has the picture, but they do not. In Quadrant 4, the vision is clear, concrete, and well understood by the whole team.

The first three quadrants are where most of the trouble lives. A quick way to find out whether you are in Quadrant 3 or Quadrant 4 is to run a short survey and ask your team how well they understand the vision. Their answers will tell you where you stand.

One of the hardest things for a leader is being clear enough for the team to follow.

There are five steps to get from a fuzzy picture to a clear one. First, accept that you are unclear. Second, figure out what is keeping the picture fuzzy. Third, ask your team and other people you trust for their honest input. Fourth, take the feedback in and work with it. Fifth, and most important, start moving. Action is the cure for a lot of confusion.

The skill of building a vision is as important to leadership as the skill of talking about it in a way that gets people excited.

Clarity is not a switch that flips on. It comes from working on the vision, sharing it in the right way, and being willing to adjust as you go.

chapters.title: A vision that moves people and gets results
chapters.text:
A clear vision is not always enough on its own. It also has to be inspiring. The Pakistani activist and Nobel laureate Malala Yousafzai showed what that looks like when she set up the Malala Fund in 2013. Her organization is a nonprofit that pays for and speaks up for education for girls in parts of the world where girls are not sent to school. The fund took off quickly, and a big reason was how clear and moving her vision was.

Malala is an inspiration in her own right. She refused to give up, even when she was threatened and shot. That kind of stubborn belief in a vision is what made the world pay attention. Celebrities and major organizations all over the world stepped up to support her cause. Her story is a useful example of why a leader and the vision have to be inspiring, not just accurate.

A big vision gives you a better shot at success than a small, safe one.

A vision also has to be practical. Practicality is what keeps you and the team going when the work gets hard. A practical vision comes with a clear plan, and that plan takes a lot of the guesswork out of the day to day. Everyone on the team knows what their job is, and that clarity is the difference between a vision that gets followed and a vision that gets forgotten.

A small note worth keeping in mind, Malala is the youngest person ever to win the Nobel Peace Prize. She was just seventeen when she received it in 2014.

chapters.title: Selling the vision across the room
chapters.text:
The best way to know whether your vision will fly is to try to sell it. If you can get your team, your board, the leaders above you, and the people below you in the company to buy in, the vision has a real chance. If you cannot sell it, it will not move.

Strong leaders pitch the vision by speaking to the real concerns of the people they are trying to reach.

Selling inside your own team comes with its own set of challenges. Some people love change, and other people do not. You have to be able to win over both kinds. Some people on the team will need to learn new skills or take on new roles, and that can create friction. Some of the feedback you get will sting, and the worst thing you can do is get defensive. Stay open, and try to see the issue from their side of the table.

When you are selling to your boss, time the conversation well. Pick a moment when they are in a good mood and ready to listen, and know when to stop talking. Once your boss says yes, say thank you and end the meeting. Do not let the win get lost in a long tail of explanation.

A small note worth tucking away, a Harris Poll found that nine in ten employees say that poor communication inside a company is the single biggest reason executives fail.

chapters.title: When people push back on your vision
chapters.text:
No vision survives without some pushback. The resistance can come from the environment around you, from the people you work with, or from inside your own head.

Environmental resistance is the most common kind in a company. Many people find it hard to try something new. New workloads, new departments, and new strategies all bring friction, and most people are not looking for extra friction in their day.

Social resistance shows up in the people around you. A well known case is Dr. Ignaz Semmelweis, who suggested that doctors wash their hands to prevent childbed fever. His boss at the hospital, Johann Klein, rejected the idea, and so did most of his colleagues. The practice only became standard after Semmelweis had died.

Vision driven and excited leaders tend to bring energy, new ideas, and growth, but they also tend to question the way things have always been done, and that is often the reason they get pushed back against.

When the resistance is in your own head, it is called psychological, and it shows up before you ever open your mouth. Doubt, the feeling that you are not good enough, and a general sense of anxiety are all part of the package. The trick is to keep those feelings from running the show.

Fear and doubt are real, but they should never be the reason a vision gets put back on the shelf.

Three traits will help you push through. Tenacity, which is the simple decision to keep going no matter what. Integrity, because a leader without integrity will lose the team, the clients, and the vision itself. And courage, which is what keeps you moving when the room is full of people who are not on your side.

chapters.title: A short look at the road ahead
chapters.text:
Vision driven leaders are the only kind of leader with the kind of energy that can move a company forward, or wake a company up before it is too late. A vision is a picture of the future, and the picture pulls new ideas and new directions out of the team.

If you want to be a vision driven leader, you have to accept that you need a clear, concrete, and compelling vision that you can actually sell. For every bit of resistance that comes your way, you have to be ready to keep walking, and you have to be willing to lean on the plan and the strategies you have built. Sometimes you have to trust your gut, because there is no map for the next step. Do whatever it takes to stay true to the vision. Do not let the fear of the unknown or the fear of failing get in the way. Vision driven leaders tend to stay steady even when the world around them is not.

A vision also has to be big enough to scare you a little. That does not mean it has to be impossible, but it has to be bigger than what is comfortable. Vision driven leaders do not think about what already exists. They think about what is missing, and they find a way to share the picture with the people who can help them build it.

Your grip on courage, your willingness to listen, and your ability to bring other people with you are the things that will carry you through the long work of being a vision driven leader.

Try this. Build a vision script of your own. Get your head clear, find a quiet spot, and picture the future you want. Write it down in as much detail as you can, and keep it somewhere you will see it often.
